elements.subsection-2411-026: Recent Increase in Allowing AI Crawlers
elements.sent-2411-519: To our surprise, a growing number of sites explicitly allowed AI crawlers in their robots.txt, welcoming AI crawlers to scrape their content.
elements.sent-2411-520: While a small number of sites fall into this unique category, the overall number of sites that explicitly allow AI crawlers is increasing over time as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:allow .}
elements.sent-2411-521: In total, 79 sites not only had no restrictions on GPTBot in their robots.txt, but also included a rule that explicitly allowed the GPTBot user agent.
elements.sent-2411-522: The data licensing agreements between OpenAI and publishers mentioned previously explain part of this increase (especially in mid-2024), but there are also other reasons.
elements.sent-2411-523: Among the sites that explicitly allow AI crawlers are popular right-wing misinformation sites, which may be motivated to spread misinformation to LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-524: Other cases are shopping sites that potentially seek to use LLMs to increase traffic to their site.
elements.sent-2411-525: Appendix~\ref{appendix:allow-robots shows the full list of sites where we observe this reverse intent toward GPTBot.}
elements.sent-2411-526: This case study highlights that sites have a variety of motives for allowing AI companies to crawl their data.
elements.section-2411-011: Sentiments and Actions of Individual Artists
elements.sent-2411-527: Section~\ref{sec:robotstxt showed that many well-resourced websites swiftly adopted robots.txt to protect their content.}
elements.sent-2411-528: In this section, we explore the question of what individual artists think about AI-related crawling and what actions they have taken in response to such crawling, if any.
elements.sent-2411-529: Compared to large organizations, individual artists have significantly more direct risk yet are comparatively under-resourced.
elements.sent-2411-530: We first present a user study, comprised of 203 professional artists, to understand their sentiments, actions, and challenges they face when dealing with AI-related crawling.
elements.sent-2411-531: While many artists are extremely concerned about AI, they lack the awareness (59\% of artists have never heard about the term ``robots.txt''), technical ability (not knowing how to use robots.txt), and agency (unable to edit robots.txt) to utilize existing technical approaches like robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-532: Informed by our user study, we follow up with a measurement study of over 1,100 artist websites to further examine the hosting services artists use and levels of control these services provide.
elements.sent-2411-533: The majority of these artists use third-party hosting services that do not allow for modification of robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-534: Among the few that do, those artists do not exercise their control, with fewer than 17\% of such artists disallowing AI-related crawlers in their robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-535: In this section we provide details on our user study, including the recruitment process, survey protocol, analysis methods, and participant demographics.. Recruitment.
elements.sent-2411-536: We conduct a user study, approved by our university's institutional review board, with professional artists.
elements.sent-2411-537: We draw participants from professional artists informed via their social circles and professional networks (e.g., internal discord channels and social media groups).
elements.sent-2411-538: We also ask participants to help distribute our survey to other artists whom they are in contact with.. Survey Protocol.
elements.sent-2411-539: We start by gathering basic information for each participant.
elements.sent-2411-540: Since our main concern is whether the artists whom we survey represent the community, we focus primarily on their artistic background (e.g., their years of experience).
elements.sent-2411-541: Next, we inquire about their knowledge and use of robots.txt, as well as their willingness to adopt robots.txt in the future.
elements.sent-2411-542: We compensate participants at a rate of \$15/hour, and the median time to complete the survey is 12 minutes.
elements.sent-2411-543: We provide our list of survey questions in Appendix~\ref{appendix:survey .. Analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-544: The first author conducted iterative open coding on the open-ended survey questions following the thematic analysis approach~\cite{braun2006using .}
elements.sent-2411-545: The segment of analysis is the entire response, which mostly consists of a few sentences.
elements.sent-2411-547: At the end, the first author created a master codebook and re-labeled the responses.
elements.sent-2411-549: After removing low quality answers (overly short or off-topic answers, straight-line answers, and incomplete answers), we obtained 203 valid responses from artists who share their artwork online.
elements.sent-2411-550: Around two thirds (136, 67\%) of the participants consider themselves as professional artists.
elements.sent-2411-551: Geographically, over 50\% of our participants are based in North America, with 80\% of them in the United States.
elements.sent-2411-552: We provide more details on the demographics of our participants in Appendix~\ref{appendix:demographics .}
elements.sent-2411-553: Echoing previous studies~\cite{bookanartistSurvey:online,DACSReport,lovato2024foregrounding,ali2023studying , surveyed artists express a strong sentiment against AI-related crawling and a strong desire for effective tools to stop it.}
elements.sent-2411-554: Over 79\% of all artists express concerns that AI-generated art will have at least moderate impact on their job security, with more than 54\% anticipating that AI art will have a significant or severe effect on their careers.
elements.sent-2411-555: A notable majority (169, 83\%) reported taking proactive measures to address these concerns.
elements.sent-2411-556: Among these 169 artists, 71\% use Glaze~\cite{shan2023glaze , a tool that employs adversarial machine learning to protect artwork.}
elements.sent-2411-557: Other common actions selected by artists include reducing the volume of work shared online and sharing lower-resolution images to mitigate potential misuse.
elements.sent-2411-558: Besides Glaze, artists mentioned alternative approaches to modify their art, such as applying watermarks or using Nightshade~\cite{shan2024nightshade .}
elements.sent-2411-559: Another common action is changing to platforms that offer better protection against AI-related crawlers and withdrawing from platforms that do not provide such protection (e.g., switching from Instagram to Cara).
elements.sent-2411-560: Lastly, a few artists mentioned that AI-generated art has impacted their career choices, with one artist stating, ``I left school and taking a gap year to reevaluate my life.'' \para{Artists would like to prevent AI crawling.}
elements.sent-2411-561: When presented with the option of a mechanism for blocking crawlers from accessing their sites, over 97\% of the artists expressed a desire to use such a mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-562: A significant majority (185, 93\%) indicated that they were ``very likely'' to adopt it.
elements.sent-2411-563: The most-commonly cited reasons included their desire to protect their work, not consenting to having their art crawled, and not being compensated for their work.
elements.sent-2411-564: Interestingly, five artists noted that such mechanisms could provide potential legal benefits (e.g., used as evidence in legal cases).
elements.sent-2411-565: The few artists who are neutral or unlikely to adopt such a mechanism cited concerns about its efficacy and trustworthiness.
elements.sent-2411-566: We observed similar but less pronounced results when we asked artists who were not familiar with robots.txt about their willingness to adopt it in the future.
elements.sent-2411-567: Concretely, 59\% of the artists (119) had not heard about robots.txt prior to our study.
elements.sent-2411-568: After reading a brief explanation of robots.txt (Appendix~\ref{appendix:survey ), almost all (113 out of 119) of the artists gained a basic understanding.\footnote{That said, we caution that many artists use terms such as ``block'' or ``stop'', while robots.txt is a voluntary mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-569: Among these artists, 75\% indicated that they would likely or very likely adopt robots.txt in the future.
elements.sent-2411-570: For those who indicated neutral or unlikely, the most common reasons cited were concerns regarding its efficacy (that robots.txt does not fully stop crawling), usability (whether it is easy to use), and the need for more information.
elements.sent-2411-571: When asked about their trust in AI companies, 77\% of participants who had not heard of robots.txt before the study expressed skepticism about AI companies respecting robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-572: Artists cited several reasons for this distrust, including the monetary incentives for AI companies to scrape data, poor track records of AI companies so far, the lack of legal enforcement, and that they perceive AI companies negatively.
elements.sent-2411-573: Experiments in Section~\ref{sec:robotsbehavior with sites we control present a more complicated picture.}
elements.sent-2411-574: Consistent with artist expectations, the majority of AI assistant crawlers do not respect robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-575: Perhaps surprisingly, though, only one major AI data crawler (Bytespider) does not respect it.
elements.sent-2411-576: Despite a strong level of distrust, 47\% of all artists remain interested in adopting, or have already adopted, robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-577: The most significant challenge is the lack of awareness among artists: as previously mentioned, around 59\% of the artists have never heard about robots.txt prior to our study.
elements.sent-2411-578: Among the 41\% who had heard of robots.txt, 90\% of them demonstrated a basic understanding of its purpose, describing it as a way of ``blocking'' or ``stopping'' crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-579: Among the 38 artists who maintain personal websites and were aware of robots.txt before the study, 27 of them have not utilized robots.txt on their personal websites.
elements.sent-2411-580: When prompted why, the single most-cited reason was not knowing how to do it.
elements.sent-2411-581: Lastly, artists reported that they do not have agency to utilize robots.txt: out of the aforementioned 38 artists, nine report having no control over the content of robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-582: Another five note the additional challenge that even though they have control over their personal website, they post on multiple platforms and can only modify the robots.txt of their personal website.
elements.sent-2411-583: The majority of these artists use third-party hosting providers that do not allow for modification of robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-584: Among the few providers that do, most artists do not exercise the option to disallow AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-585: We identified the personal websites of artists using directories of two top artist associations in the U.S., Concept Art Association and Animation Union.
elements.sent-2411-586: In total, we collected a list of 1,182 sites.
elements.sent-2411-587: The majority of these artists (over 78\%) use one of eight hosting providers, such as Squarespace and ArtStation, to host their websites, followed by a long tail of small providers, self-hosted websites, and social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-588: As such, we focus on the top eight hosting providers in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-589: Most of these platforms provide drag-and-drop tools, allowing artists to easily upload their portfolios and personal information.
elements.sent-2411-590: As well, many artists obtain custom domain names through these services for an additional fee.
elements.sent-2411-591: To determine which hosting provider an artist's website uses, we rely on DNS.
elements.sent-2411-592: In some cases (e.g., Carbonmade), the artist sites are subdomains of their provider (e.g., example.carbonmade.com).
elements.sent-2411-593: For sites hosted on Wix, their domains allow for straightforward differentiation between free and paid versions: sites hosted using the free version of Wix use subdomains of wix.com, whereas sites using the paid version have a registered domain whose DNS record points to Wix's infrastructure.

elements.sent-2411-594: Hosting providers give limited control and information to artists.
elements.sent-2411-595: Table~\ref{tab:webhostingresults shows the services used by artists, usage percentage, and percentage of websites that disallow any AI crawlers (Table~\ref{tab:ai_agents ) in their robots.txt.}}
elements.sent-2411-596: The contents of robots.txt files are identical for all artists who host with a particular hosting provider except artists who use Squarespace.
elements.sent-2411-597: To better understand the agency these hosting providers give their users, we registered accounts with each of them.

elements.sent-2411-599: Out of these four, only Carbonmade disallows AI crawlers (GPTBot and CCBot) in their default robots.txt file.

elements.sent-2411-602: This option adds full restrictions on ten AI user agents, including GPTBot and anthropic-ai (the full list is available in Appendix~\ref{appendix:squarespacebots ).}
elements.sent-2411-603: We also investigated if any of these providers actively block AI crawlers in addition to disallowing them in robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-604: While all providers state that they do not claim ownership over user content, only Adobe~\cite{adobeTOS and Artstation~\cite{artstationTOS explicitly mention in their terms of service that they do not use or license user content for generative AI training.}}
elements.sent-2411-605: On the other hand, Wix can use user content to train their AI tools, but only for the purpose of ``maintain[ing] and improv[ing] the Services''~\cite{wixTOS .}
elements.sent-2411-606: Finally, while Carbonmade does not mention AI training in their terms of service, they have a clause prohibiting crawling content on their site: ``obtain[ing] or attempt[ing] to obtain any materials, documents or information through any means not purposely made available through the website'' is prohibited~\cite{carbonmadeTOS .}
elements.sent-2411-607: We next examine to what extent artists actively utilize these options.
elements.sent-2411-608: For Wix's paid version, which provides the highest level of control over the robots.txt file, none of the 1,100 websites in our dataset had edited their robots.txt file.
elements.sent-2411-609: When attempting to modify the file through our paid Wix account, we discover that the interface is confusing and found it difficult to determine how to make changes.
elements.sent-2411-610: In contrast, Squarespace offers a very straightforward option: a single button that allows users to disallow AI access.
elements.sent-2411-611: However, only 49 (17\%) of the 293 artists who use Squarespace had enabled this option --- a figure significantly lower than the 75\% of artists who, in our user study, expressed a desire to disallow AI crawlers when given the choice.
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=1.0\columnwidth]{plot/squarespace.png} \caption{Squarespace provides a user-friendly option for controlling whether AI-related crawlers are disallowed in a site's robots.txt.} \label{fig:squarespace}
elements.sent-2411-612: We hypothesize that the significant gap between the large percentage of artists desiring to take action and the small percentage who actually do so is due to two main reasons.
elements.sent-2411-613: Figure~\ref{fig:squarespace shows a screenshot of the information provided to users, which lacks any mention of robots.txt or details on which AI crawlers are included.}
elements.sent-2411-614: It states, ``your site won't be scanned to train AI models'' --- an ambiguous claim, as the feature only modifies the robots.txt file and does not prevent all scanning or data usage by AI.
elements.sent-2411-615: Since robots.txt is a voluntary mechanism, Web crawlers do not have to respect it.
elements.sent-2411-616: Indeed, anecdotal evidence has suggested that some crawlers appear to ignore robots.txt~\cite{PerplexityIgnore:online,ClaudeBotIgnore:online,BytedanceIgnore:online,IgnoreBulk:online .}
elements.sent-2411-617: Further complicating the issue is the recent emergence of AI assistant crawlers that fetch pages for generative models --- these crawlers are triggered by user queries, a use case not clearly covered by the robots.txt standard.
elements.sent-2411-618: In this section, we explore the question of whether AI crawlers respect robots.txt files.
elements.sent-2411-619: The results are nuanced: the majority of the AI crawlers operated by big companies do respect robots.txt, while the majority of AI assistant crawlers do not.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{plot/gptapp_eg.png} \caption{Example of a GPT app (WebG) that can retrieve information from the Web. Upon clicking ``Allow'', WebG can retrieve information via mixerbox.com.} \label{fig:gptapp_eg}
elements.sent-2411-620: In this section, we describe how we setup our website, followed by how we conducted our measurements.. Experiment setup.
elements.sent-2411-621: To determine whether crawlers respect robots.txt, we created two websites with different robots.txt files.
elements.sent-2411-622: The first website has a robots.txt file that disallows all crawlers using the wildcard rule ``User-agent: *; Disallow: /''.
elements.sent-2411-623: The second website has a robots.txt file that disallows AI crawlers by listing every user agent individually (e.g., ``User-agent: Amazonbot; Disallow: /'').
elements.sent-2411-624: Both websites contain basic text, images, and links to other pages.
elements.sent-2411-625: We host them on a cloud provider with the same IP address, create valid certificates, and log all requests.
elements.sent-2411-626: We link to both websites from various pages under our control (e.g., personal websites) to increase the chances of crawlers visiting them.. Passive measurement.
elements.sent-2411-627: Using these sites we conduct a passive measurement study for six months from September 2024 to March 2025.
elements.sent-2411-628: Concretely, we passively wait for crawlers to visit our website.
elements.sent-2411-629: Later, we use user agent and IP addresses (if available) to identify individual AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-630: For AI crawlers that do not document the list of IP addresses they use, we search the Internet to make sure that the IP addresses we observe are commonly associated with the crawlers (e.g., others have observed traffic from the same /24 with the same user agent).. Active measurement.
elements.sent-2411-631: We also conduct an active measurement study in November 2024.
elements.sent-2411-632: We actively request AI assistant crawlers to visit our websites and observe if they respect the robots.txt file.
elements.sent-2411-633: To this end, we compile a list of AI assistant crawlers for which we can trigger visits to our website.
elements.sent-2411-634: This list includes built-in AI assistant crawlers that are part of ChatGPT and Meta's LLAMA.
elements.sent-2411-635: In addition, apps in ChatGPT's store (also known as GPT apps) can also retrieve information from the Web using crawlers operated by third parties.
elements.sent-2411-636: We consider these third-party crawlers as AI assistant crawlers, too.
elements.sent-2411-637: Figure~\ref{fig:gptapp_eg shows an example of a GPT app (WebG) that can retrieve information through the crawler operated by mixerbox.com.}
elements.sent-2411-638: To create a list of such crawlers, we start by examining a list of the top 5\K GPT apps listed on GPTStore (a popular website cited in various prior efforts that study GPT apps~\cite{su2024gpt, zhang2024first,hou2024security ).}
elements.sent-2411-639: We then interact with each GPT app in an automated manner to determine whether it can retrieve information from the Web by asking it to visit a website we control.
elements.sent-2411-640: We use two different prompts: (a) ``Start action, fetch page: [url]''; and (b) ``Get web page content: [url].'' We check that a request is made to our website by examining the server logs.
elements.sent-2411-641: Next, we identify individual crawlers that make these requests using a combination of domain and IP address information.
elements.sent-2411-642: Concretely, we examine the domain contacted by each GPT app (e.g., WebG contacts mixerbox.com in Figure~\ref{fig:gptapp_eg ) and the IP address that each crawler uses to visit our website.}
elements.sent-2411-643: We merge any crawlers that share at least one IP address or has the same registered domain name.
elements.sent-2411-644: This process yields 23 distinct third-party AI assistant crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-645: We start by presenting the results of our passive measurement, followed by the results of our active measurement.
elements.sent-2411-646: Most of the crawlers that visited our websites respect the robots.txt file (Table~\ref{tab:ai_agents ).}
elements.sent-2411-647: During our six-month measurement period, nine AI crawlers visited our websites without our request: Amazonbot, Applebot, Bytespider, CCBot, ChatGPT-User, ClaudeBot, GPTBot, Meta-ExternalAgent, and OAI-SearchBot, most of which are AI data crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-648: Seven crawlers (Amazonbot, Applebot, CCBot, ClaudeBot, GPTBot, Meta-ExternalAgent, and OAI-SearchBot) respected the robots.txt file.
elements.sent-2411-649: One crawler (Bytespider) fetched the robots.txt file but did not respect it.
elements.sent-2411-650: ChatGPT-User visited our website once and did not fetch the robots.txt file, which contradicts its behavior in our active measurement.
elements.sent-2411-651: Given that it is a user-triggered crawler and we did not trigger it, it is unclear why this crawler visited our website.\footnote{We also verified that the IP and user-agent are indeed associated with OpenAI.}
elements.sent-2411-652: Both ChatGPT's and Facebook's built-in AI assistant crawlers respected the robots.txt file.
elements.sent-2411-653: ChatGPT's crawler can be identified with the user agent ``ChatGPT-User'' while Meta uses a mix of ``FacebookExternalHit'' and ``Meta-ExternalAgent'' as the user agent.
elements.sent-2411-654: Both ChatGPT and Meta start by requesting robots.txt from a website.
elements.sent-2411-655: If the robots.txt file disallows the crawler, the crawler will not fetch content on the website.
elements.sent-2411-656: Interestingly, according to both the official documentation~\cite{metaCrawlers and Dark Visitors~\cite{darkvisitorsagents , Meta's AI assistant crawler should use the user agent ``Meta-ExternalFetcher''.}}
elements.sent-2411-657: However, we do not observe any crawler with this user agent in either our passive or active measurements.
elements.sent-2411-658: Instead, our observation is that Meta uses ``FacebookExternalHit'' or ``Meta-ExternalAgent'' for both AI data crawling (training) and AI assistant crawling (user-triggered).
elements.sent-2411-659: For the 23 third-party crawlers, most of them did not respect the robots.txt file: one crawler fetched and respected robots.txt files; one has a bug in its implementation that caused it to incorrectly fetch the robots.txt file; one did not fetch the robots.txt file most of the time; and the remaining 20 crawlers did not fetch the robots.txt file at all (and hence do not respect it).
elements.sent-2411-660: The effectiveness of a mechanism like robots.txt depends both on the ability of content owners to express their intent to prevent crawling, as well as the willingness of AI companies to respect the prohibitions that content creators have expressed.
elements.sent-2411-661: In this section we explore active blocking as another option for protecting content from AI crawling.
elements.sent-2411-662: We first measure the prevalence of active blocking on popular sites.
elements.sent-2411-663: Then, as a case study we comprehensively evaluate the AI-specific active blocking option provided by Cloudflare.
elements.sent-2411-664: Active blocking is largely overlooked as a content access control mechanism in prior work~\cite{dinzinger2024survey,longpre2024consent,dinzinger2024longitudinal,fletcher2024many , so its adoption for this purpose is relatively unknown.}
elements.sent-2411-665: Hence, we first explore its use by estimating the proportion of popular websites that actively block AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-666: In particular, we estimate the use of active blocking on the top 10\K websites in the most-recent Tranco ranking in our dataset (October 2024).
elements.sent-2411-667: For simplicity, we opted for a user-agent based approach (inspired by~\cite{pham2016understanding ) to detect active blocking.}
elements.sent-2411-668: With this approach we visit sites with different user agents (a common default user agent vs.\ AI crawlers) and compare the results.
elements.sent-2411-669: A site that actively blocks based on an AI user agent will return very different content compared to accessing the site with a common user agent.
elements.sent-2411-670: We acknowledge that many advanced bot detection methods exist (e.g., through fingerprinting or behavioral analysis), and consider our results a conservative estimate of the overall number of sites that actively block AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-671: Following~\cite{pham2016understanding , for each website we perform the following steps: Control case: We first identify sites that inherently block our automation tool, regardless of the user agent.}
elements.sent-2411-672: In these cases, we cannot distinguish whether a site is blocking our tool, or is blocking based on a particular user agent.
elements.sent-2411-673: We visit the site with a headless browser (Chromium automated by Selenium) and set its user agent to a typical Chrome user with the OS matching the machine the browser runs on.
elements.sent-2411-674: If a site returns a non-200 HTTP status code (after any potential redirections), we make no inferences on its use of active blocking of AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-675: By excluding these sites, we again consider our measurement of the active blocking adoption rate to be a lower bound.
elements.sent-2411-676: AI case: Holding all else constant, we then revisit all sites that do not block our tool with two Anthropic user agents: Claudebot and anthropic-ai.
elements.sent-2411-677: We use just these two AI user agents because, according to Dark Visitors~\cite{darkvisitorsagents , these are the two most-frequently restricted AI user agents that do not have published IP address origins.}
elements.sent-2411-678: Since Anthropic does not publish the IP address ranges it uses for crawling, site operators would more likely actively block them based on user agent.
elements.sent-2411-679: The companies associated with the other AI user agents do publish IP address ranges, and sites could actively block based solely on the IP address of the crawler --- a form of active blocking that we cannot measure.
elements.sent-2411-680: Detecting blocking behavior based on user agent: To identify active blocking, we check the HTTP status code, any exceptions that occur, and whether there are significant differences in the HTTP content length returned (inspired by~\cite{jones2014automated ).\footnote{\revision{For sites where we observed a difference in HTTP content length (but the same HTTP status code) between the ``Control'' and ``AI'' crawls, we manually validated that these were in fact cases where the site returned a ``block'' page instead of some trivial difference.}}}
elements.sent-2411-681: Any differences in these features between the ``Control'' and ``AI'' crawls indicate active blocking based on the AI user agent.
elements.sent-2411-682: Using this methodology, we infer that 1,433 (14\%) of the top 10\K October 2024 sites actively block two of Anthropic's AI crawlers, indicating that active blocking, like robots.txt, is a relatively established content access-control mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-683: Many sites use active blocking instead of robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-684: Only 35 (2\%) of the 1,433 top 10\K sites that actively block anthropic-ai and Claudebot also have explicit restrictions on these user agents in robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-685: The very limited use of robots.txt among these sites indicates that many sites indeed use active blocking as their sole form of restriction on AI crawling.
elements.sent-2411-686: However, active blocking cannot replace robots.txt for all AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-687: While active blocking may seem like a strictly better alternative, it inherently cannot replace some directives in robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-688: Specifically, in the case where companies use the same crawler to collect content for both AI training as well as for other purposes (e.g., indexing for Web search), active blocking is an all-or-nothing approach that can have unwanted side-effects.
elements.sent-2411-689: Examples of these mixed-use crawlers include Google's Googlebot and Apple's Applebot: blocking them completely can have severe consequences on a site's visibility in search indexes.
elements.sent-2411-690: The only way for users to allow crawling for search indexing and opt out of AI training for these companies is to add a disallow directive for a special ``dummy'' user agent (Google-Extended and Applebot-Extended) to robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-691: This mechanism, while ad-hoc, highlights that robots.txt is indeed still necessary even with active blocking measures in place.
elements.sent-2411-692: While some active blocking configurations require the user to manually input the blocking rules (e.g., through Apache's .htaccess), other active blocking tools (such as third-party bot-detection platforms) act as black boxes for users, leaving them unaware of its exact behavior (e.g., which user agents are blocked).
elements.sent-2411-693: If the list of AI user agents is incomplete, for example, it can \revision{mislead the user into believing their content is fully protected .}
elements.sent-2411-694: We end by noting that for a comprehensive approach to prevent AI crawling, it is important for site owners to still use robots.txt in conjunction with active blocking and verify that their active blocking configuration matches their expectations.
elements.sent-2411-695: As a case study of third-party active blocking, we examine Cloudflare's recently-launched Block AI Bots feature~\cite{cloudflareAIblocking .}
elements.sent-2411-696: It is a compelling feature to evaluate because Cloudflare is currently the only third-party service that offers any AI-specific active blocking mechanism, it is a highly popular service~\cite{reverseproxymarketshare , and this feature is clearly targeted toward a less technically-proficient user base.}
elements.sent-2411-697: While the feature is designed to be user-friendly (a ``single click''), its operation is unfortunately a black box to the user.
elements.sent-2411-698: Based on this understanding, we then estimate its adoption among the 2,018 (20\%) sites of the Tranco top 10\K that are hosted on Cloudflare in October 2024.. Grey-box evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-699: To evaluate its operation, we created an account with Cloudflare and configured their reverse proxy service on a website we control.
elements.sent-2411-700: While Cloudflare states that its Block AI Bots feature is available for all payment tiers, for validation we tested both the free and ``Pro'' tiers.
elements.sent-2411-701: Inferring the list of AI user agents covered.
elements.sent-2411-702: Cloudflare does not document the list of AI crawlers they block under this new feature.
elements.sent-2411-703: Thus, to infer its coverage, we send requests to our own website with the AI user agents in Table~\ref{tab:ai_agents and an additional 590 user agents from a public list of crawlers~\cite{crawleruseragents .\footnote{The GitHub repository we used includes the full user-agent string, which is important to note in case a service uses specific pattern matching.}}}
elements.sent-2411-704: We first make a request with the Block AI Bots option turned off, and another with it on.
elements.sent-2411-705: For these paired requests, we determine whether or not a given user agent was blocked using the HTTP response codes and the dashboard for our Cloudflare account.
elements.sent-2411-706: In all, Cloudflare's feature blocks 17 AI user agents, as shown in Appendix~\ref{cloudflareblockaiscraperslist .}
elements.sent-2411-707: Inferring the adoption of Cloudflare's Block AI Bots option.
elements.sent-2411-708: Figure~\ref{fig:cloudflare_inference shows the logic we used to infer whether a website using Cloudflare has turned on the Block AI Bots setting.}
elements.sent-2411-709: Cloudflare also has another managed ruleset, called Definitely Automated, that covers all the unverified\footnote{The verified AI bots include Amazonbot, Applebot (which is not blocked), GPTBot, OAI-SearchBot (not blocked), ChatGPT-User, ICC Crawler (not blocked), and DuckAssistbot (not blocked).}
elements.sent-2411-710: For more details on the operation of this setting, see Appendix~\ref{appendix:cloudflaredefautomated .}
elements.sent-2411-712: As with the popular sites, we used the ClaudeBot and anthropic-ai user agents as they are not Cloudflare verified bots and do not publish or document their IP address origins, so it is unlikely that Cloudflare uses IP addresses to check for requests from these two crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-713: As for inferring the Definitely Automated option, we chose the user agents of two less popular web automation libraries that are blocked by the managed rule (HeadlessChrome and libwww-perl), reducing the chance that a website has configured some custom blocking rule against one of them.
elements.sent-2411-714: We inspect the HTTP response code and the returned HTML content to detect whether a Cloudflare Block or Challenge page was returned, or if the site content was returned (indicating the user agent was not blocked).
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{plot/Cloudflare-Inference-FINAL.pdf} \caption{Flowchart for inferring the Block AI Bots setting on websites hosted by Cloudflare.} \label{fig:cloudflare_inference} \vspace*{-0.6cm}
elements.sent-2411-715: We conclusively determined the setting for 1,875 (93\%)\footnote{For the remaining sites, we were unable to determine the setting as they may have been using third-party blocking mechanisms, or have some custom, non-standard Cloudflare Web Application Firewall configuration.}
elements.sent-2411-716: Of these 1,875 sites, only 107 (5.7\%) sites enable Cloudflare's Block AI Bots option.
elements.sent-2411-717: Yet, these sites also disallow AI-related crawlers in their robots.txt files at a much higher rate than average: 24\% as opposed to 12\% among the other Cloudflare sites that do not enable the Block AI Bots option.
elements.sent-2411-718: These 107 sites show a strong intent to block AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-719: To sum up, while the active blocking feature provided by Cloudflare may not be widely used yet, but it is an encouraging new option.
elements.sent-2411-720: Like all measurement studies, ours has limitations in scope, methodology and generalizability.. \para{Scope of participants.}
elements.sent-2411-721: The user study included 203 professional artists, which does not fully represent the entire population of content creators.
elements.sent-2411-722: In particular, most participants were based in North America, which limits the coverage of creators from other countries.
elements.sent-2411-723: In our dataset, robots.txt files were collected by Common Crawl or our own custom crawler.
elements.sent-2411-724: A percentage of sites returned non-200 responses and were excluded from our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-725: Our estimation of the adoption rate of active blocking presented in Section~\ref{subsec:activeBlockGeneral is a conservative lower bound since or 15\% of the sites tested, we could not determine their active blocking behavior due to our crawler being blocked independent of the user agent used.}
elements.sent-2411-726: In Section~\ref{subsec:cloudflareCaseStudy , we assume that a site does not configure any custom active blocking rules against the user agents we use.}
elements.sent-2411-727: For example, for a small proportion of sites we determined they were using an additional active blocking service (e.g., PerimeterX).
elements.sent-2411-728: We excluded those sites from our analysis.. \para{Single measurement.}
elements.sent-2411-729: Finally, our study represents measurements from both a point in time and with a particular methodology.
elements.sent-2411-730: Thus, the behaviors that we document may have been different in the past, may yet change in the future, and may even vary based on factors such as country of origin.
elements.sent-2411-731: Instead, a series of ad hoc controls have emerged based on repurposing existing Web norms and firewall capabilities, none of which match the specificity, usability, or level of enforcement that is, in fact, desired by content creators.
elements.sent-2411-732: We believe there exist four kinds of issues that limit the value of these protections in practice: ambiguity, respect for signal, user control, and legal uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-733: Perhaps unsurprisingly, robots.txt is an imperfect mechanism for this purpose and introduces a range of ambiguities --- even for the purpose of measurement --- around what robots.txt means and how it is honored.. Syntactic ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-734: One source of such ambiguity is the syntactic and lexical structure of robots.txt, which is unintuitively complex.
elements.sent-2411-735: As a result, different parsers interpret the same set of directives differently.
elements.sent-2411-736: For example, the parser used in~\cite{longpre2024consent misinterprets grouping rules and also mistakenly treats the \verb|User-agent| line as case-sensitive, leading to large numbers of disallow directives being ignored.}
elements.sent-2411-737: Similarly, robots.txt authors themselves can misunderstand the syntactic requirements of the protocol.
elements.sent-2411-738: Approximately 1\% of sites we studied have mistakes in their robots.txt (e.g., such as not starting a path with a ``/'' or using non-existent directives).. Naming ambiguity.
elements.sent-2411-739: However, a more significant problem is that robots.txt's ability to specify that LLM-training crawlers are unwelcome is predicated on the notion that the purpose of a crawler is clearly and uniquely identified via the user agent string.
elements.sent-2411-740: Moreover, keeping track of the current user agent mapping for all such crawlers is a burden placed on each site administrator.
elements.sent-2411-741: Lastly, a number of crawlers serve dual purposes: gathering data that is used both for updating search indexes and for training AI models.
elements.sent-2411-742: Thus, a site owner wishing to prevent their content being acquired for AI training may be faced with a difficult tradeoff as their desire to block a crawler may also force them to forgo the benefits of appearing in a popular search index.\footnote{This is similar to the ambiguity problem that arises in the use of IP blocklisting --- a single server may host benign and offending content.}
elements.sent-2411-743: Some such ``dual-purpose'' organizations have documented particular AI-specific ``tokens'' (e.g., Applebot-Extended and Google-Extended) that may be included in robots.txt as a signal for sites to indicate that the content gathered by their crawlers should not be used for training by the associated organization.
elements.sent-2411-744: However, this ``opt-out'' signal is far from standard and operates at the discretion of the crawling organization (i.e., it does not stop the acquisition of content, but only signals the site's preference for its use).
elements.sent-2411-745: Thus, any subsequent changes in policy or interpretation are at the sole discretion of the crawling organization.\footnote{Indeed, there is some evidence that the original version of the Googlebot-Extended signal did not exclude the use of content in training Google's Search Generative Experience search results~\cite{googlebotextended2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-747: The Robots Exclusion Protocol does not make clear what a ``robot'' is, and each organization can make its own interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-748: For example, Google's documented policy is that robots.txt is not applicable to crawlers controlled by users (for example, feed subscriptions).
elements.sent-2411-749: Indeed, there are few norms about whether user-triggered fetches should be exempt from the protocol, even when such fetches may themselves be driven by a generative AI.
elements.sent-2411-750: For example, Meta's user-triggered crawler Meta-ExternalFetcher and Perplexity's recently-announced Perplexity-User~\cite{perplexity-user both claim to ignore robots.txt.}
elements.sent-2411-751: In contrast, OpenAI takes the opposite approach with ChatGPT-User, which obeys robots.txt.
elements.sent-2411-752: As we have shown in Section~\ref{sec:robotsbehavior , not all crawlers respect robots.txt (e.g., ByteDance's Bytespider ignores robots.txt directives) and others, while they abide, may cache robots.txt and may continue to fetch content even after it has changed.}
elements.sent-2411-753: At the extreme, some crawlers may pretend to be regular user browsers, thus necessitating the use of advanced active blocking techniques such as fingerprinting~\cite{cloudflareAIblocking .}
elements.sent-2411-754: In comparison, active blocking (e.g., as offered by Cloudflare) allows better enforcement of an access policy, but still suffers from issues such as dual-purpose crawlers and fetches laundered via a third-party infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-755: In addition, some LLM crawlers do not use identifiable ranges of IP addresses and thus IP-level blocking is not technically feasible (e.g., Anthropic~\cite{anthropicblock ).}
elements.sent-2411-756: This reversal shows that large content owners are willing to let their data be used for AI training, but only if they receive monetary compensation and/or site traffic\footnote{For example, in the deal between OpenAI and Dotdash Meredith, one contract term requires that OpenAI must link to their site when displaying information relevant to one of their subsidiaries~\cite{dotdashDataDeal .}}
elements.sent-2411-757: Since few creators maintain their own Web server, they must rely on their website hoster to provide an interface to such capabilities that creators can understand and is technically effective.
elements.sent-2411-758: However, few hosters export robots.txt directly to their customers and most do not provide any separate mechanism to express a desire to block AI bots.
elements.sent-2411-759: Finally, if a third party copies a creator's content (e.g., posts it on social media) no anti-crawler protections follow this content to its new host.
elements.sent-2411-760: Thus, to the extent creator control is possible, it may be limited to direct accesses by AI crawlers.
elements.sent-2411-761: While this paper has focused on technical data access restrictions, it is within a larger legal context about the extent to which copyright holders will have an effective remedy if their content is accessed and integrated into AI models without their consent.
elements.sent-2411-762: This situation is complicated by a landscape that differs across geographic regions.
elements.sent-2411-763: In the US, this question is being litigated in the courts, primarily around the extent that the models trained on copyrighted data are derived works and if commercial AI companies can avail themselves of the ``first use'' doctrine to bypass traditional obligations to copyright holders for derived works.
elements.sent-2411-764: By contrast, the EU has no general-purpose fair use exception, and while there are text and data-mining exceptions, the EU's recent AI Act makes clear (via Recital 105) that ``where the rights to opt out has been expressly reserved in an appropriate manner, providers of general-purpose AI models need to obtain an authorization from rightsholders if they want to carry out text and data mining over such works''~\cite{AIAct .}
elements.sent-2411-765: Yet other countries have instead liberalized their copyright policies specifically to support the AI industry.
elements.sent-2411-766: For example, Singapore's copyright law now includes an exception for the purpose of ``computational data analysis'' (Section 244~\cite{SingaporeCA ) and Japan's law has also been amended to allow exploitation of copyrighted works in which ``it is not a person's purpose to personally enjoy or cause another person to enjoy'' the work (Article 30-4~\cite{JapanCA ).}}
elements.sent-2411-767: However, even in these more permissive legal environments the precise line for when such activity crosses into unprotected use is unclear.
elements.sent-2411-768: Indeed, there is reason to believe that confusion around the availability of legal remedies will only further focus attention on technical access controls such as those we have discussed.
elements.sent-2411-769: For example, the ``in an appropriate manner'' opt-out provisions of the EU's AI Act are not prescriptive and will inevitably engage with the challenges we have discussed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-770: Similarly to the extent that any US court finds an affirmative ``fair use'' defense for AI model builders, this weakening of remedies on use will inevitably create an even stronger demand to enforce controls on access.
elements.sent-2411-771: In summary, our work highlights the challenges for today's content creators with respect to AI use.
elements.sent-2411-772: First, there are no existing standard mechanisms for explicitly controlling whether publicly-accessible Web content is used in training AI models.
elements.sent-2411-773: Second, the existing mechanisms that have been brought to bear for this purpose are poor fits for the task, lack appropriate specificity, comprehensiveness or verifiability.
elements.sent-2411-774: Third, these mechanisms are generally not readily available to individual content creators and more serve the interests of large organizations.
elements.sent-2411-775: Last but not least, uncertainty and differences exist around the legal protections for content creators.

elements.sent-2411-785: When experimenting with robots.txt parsers from both Google and~\cite{longpre2024consent , we discovered three edge cases that can lead to very different interpretations of a robots.txt file depending on whether a parser is fully compliant with RFC 9309.. \para{Case 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-786: For the following robots.txt, a compliant parser will ignore comments or newlines after the ``User-agent'' line and respect the ``Disallow'' directives.
elements.sent-2411-787: If a parser does not handle such comments or newlines correctly, the parser may skip and ignore the ``Disallow'' directives:.
elements.sent-2411-788: RFC 9309 allows ``User-agent'' directives to be grouped as shown below.
elements.sent-2411-789: A non-compliant parser, however, can ignore all such grouped ``User-agent'' lines except for the last when parsing robots.txt:.
elements.verbatim-2411-002: User-agent: GPTBot User-agent: anthropic-ai User-agent: Claudebot Disallow: /
elements.sent-2411-790: Using unsupported directives can have unintended consequences.
elements.sent-2411-791: For example, ``Crawl-delay'' is a non-standard extension supported by some crawlers and ignored by others, a situation that can lead to unexpected results depending on the parser used by the crawler.
elements.sent-2411-792: Google's compliant robots.txt parser will ignore the ``Crawl-delay'' directive and effectively treat it as a blank line.
elements.sent-2411-793: As a result, in the following robots.txt the ``User-agent: *'' directive will be combined with the ``User-agent: GoogleBot'' directive due to the grouping rule (ignoring ``Crawl-delay'' and effectively grouping the two ``User-agent'' lines together):.
elements.verbatim-2411-003: User-agent: * Disallow: / User-agent: * Crawl-delay: 5 User-agent: GoogleBot Allow: / Disallow: /z/
elements.sent-2411-794: In contrast, a parser that obeys the non-standard ``Crawl-delay'' directive will not group together the two ``User-agent'' lines (only the GoogleBot user agent will be associated with the two ``Allow/Disallow'' rules).
